title: A Thermodynamic Analysis of Enhanced Metastability in Isochoric Supercooled Liquids
abstract: Experiments show that isochoric (constant-volume) conditions enhance supercooling stability relative to isobaric (constant-pressure) conditions. Here, combining Helmholtz equilibrium thermodynamics with a first-order perturbation methodology, we derive an inequality governing nucleation stability under volumetric constraint. The derivation provides a general thermodynamic proof that for any substance undergoing phase transformation in which the solid is less dense than the liquid, the Helmholtz driving force for solidification in isochoric systems is smaller than the Gibbs driving force in isobaric systems. Since nucleation rates depend exponentially on the inverse square of the driving force, this provides a thermodynamic basis for the observed suppression of nucleation rates. While a full stochastic treatment is beyond the scope of this work, the reduction in driving force implies a weakening of the bias toward growth of pre-critical fluctuations, increasing their probability of thermal dissolution. The analysis yields a dimensionless isochoric stability number. This number is computable from bulk thermodynamic data alone and provides a geometry-independent criterion for comparing metastable liquid stability across materials and conditions.
summary: This paper provides a rigorous thermodynamic proof that constant-volume conditions enhance the stability of supercooled liquids compared to constant-pressure conditions. By showing that the expansion of a less-dense solid generates a self-limiting pressure rise, the author demonstrates a reduction in the thermodynamic driving force for nucleation. This reduction leads to an exponential suppression of nucleation rates, providing a universal, geometry-independent basis for the observed stability. The work introduces a new dimensionless parameter, the isochoric stability number, to compare this effect across different materials.
main_problem: To provide a general thermodynamic explanation for why isochoric (constant-volume) supercooling is more stable against nucleation than isobaric (constant-pressure) conditions.
main_result: The paper proves that for substances where the solid is less dense than the liquid, the Helmholtz driving force for solidification is strictly smaller than the Gibbs driving force, leading to an exponential suppression of nucleation rates.
method: The author uses Helmholtz equilibrium thermodynamics combined with a first-order perturbation methodology, expanding thermodynamic variables in terms of a small solid mass fraction.
model_or_system: The study focuses on supercooled liquids undergoing phase transformation to a less dense solid (such as water, silicon, or gallium) under isochoric confinement.
key_observables: The Helmholtz driving force, the Gibbs driving force, and the dimensionless isochoric stability number (Pi_0).
important_parameters: The isochoric stability number (Pi_0), the Clapeyron slope, liquid isothermal compressibility, and the nascent solid mass fraction (xi).
main_assumptions: The derivation assumes a negative Clapeyron slope (v_s > v_l) and utilizes a first-order approximation in the solid fraction, neglecting higher-order terms.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the P-T phase diagram and the prohibition of complete solidification under isochoric conditions; Figure 2 illustrates the Helmholtz free energy T-V phase diagram and the common tangent construction for equilibrium pressure.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of isochoric stability, develops a thermodynamic framework using Helmholtz free energy, derives a pressure-induced melting point depression, establishes a thermodynamic inequality for driving forces, and introduces a dimensionless stability criterion.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Figure 2. Helmholtz free energy T–V phase diagram of water. (A) Free energy surfaces of liquid water and ice Ih as functions of temperature and specific volume; the common tangent construction (Lines of Dissipated Energy) gives the equilibrium pressure. (B) Projected T–V phase diagram with broad two-phase equilibrium region traversed by pressure isoclines. Nucleation in an isochoric system traces a vertical path; the equilibrium phase fraction is determined by the Lever rule. The arrow shows a slow equilibrium decompression path.
